# Emergence: a working synthesis

Emergence names the way a system's higher-level behaviour cannot be read off from
its parts in isolation. Convection cells in heated fluid, flocking in starlings,
and price formation in a market all share a signature: many simple local
interactions, repeated, settle into a global pattern that none of the individual
units represents or intends. The pattern is real — you can measure the roll cells,
the flock's polarization, the market-clearing price — yet it lives at a different
scale than the rules that produce it.

Two distinctions keep the idea honest. First, *weak* emergence (the cells, the
flock) is in principle derivable by simulating the parts forward; *strong*
emergence claims an in-principle gap, which remains contested and which I do not
assume. Second, emergence is not magic: it is what you get when feedback couples
local state to a shared field, so each unit responds to a summary the whole
produces. That coupling is the mechanism, and it is where intervention has
leverage — change the coupling and the global pattern shifts, even with the units
untouched.
